1:
"It is reported commonly that there is fornication among you, and such fornication as is not so much as named among the Gentiles, that one should have his father's wife." The witnesses note the report of gross immorality in the church. Chrysostom notes that, unlike his gentler approach to their divisions, here Paul lays about him immediately and makes the reproach as general as possible — not singling out the offender alone, but charging the whole church. The witnesses note Paul reporting the common knowledge of fornication among the Corinthians — and such as was not even named among the Gentiles, a man having his father's wife (incest); the scandalous sin in the church, a wickedness exceeding even the heathen's, openly known and tolerated; the gross immorality that demanded discipline; the scandal of the church. The witnesses note fornication among you. The witnesses note not named among the Gentiles.

The witnesses note the report of gross immorality — incest not even named among the Gentiles — the scandalous sin tolerated in the church.

So the witnesses make the verse the report of gross immorality. The lesson the expositors draw is the scandal of a wickedness in the church exceeding even the heathen's, openly known and demanding discipline. The witnesses note the report: "it is reported commonly that there is fornication among you" — the fornication was commonly reported, openly known; the report; the common knowledge; the scandal known; the sin reported; the public notoriety. The expositors observe the aggravation: "such fornication as is not so much as named among the Gentiles, that one should have his father's wife" — the fornication was incest (a man with his father's wife, his stepmother) — a sin not even named (tolerated, practised) among the Gentiles; the aggravation; the incest; the sin exceeding the heathen's; the gross wickedness; the unnamed crime. The witnesses press the exceeding of the heathen: the sin exceeded even the Gentiles' — a wickedness the very heathen abhorred; the exceeding; the worse than the heathen; the sin even pagans condemned; the gross immorality; the shame the greater. The expositors draw the lesson on the scandal in the church: that such a sin was found in the church — and tolerated — was a great scandal; the scandal; the sin in the church; the dishonour to the gospel; the reproach; the church shamed. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's note on the general reproach: Chrysostom notes Paul makes the reproach general — charging not the offender alone, but the whole church for tolerating it; the general reproach; the whole church charged; the toleration rebuked; the corporate guilt; the church responsible. The expositors note the demand for discipline: the gross sin, openly known and tolerated, demanded discipline — the offender to be put away; the demand; the discipline required; the sin to be dealt with; the purging needed; the action called for. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the fornication among you, the not named among the Gentiles, and the scandal demanding discipline. The verse thus records the report of gross immorality — teaching the scandal of a wickedness in the church exceeding the heathen's, openly known and demanding discipline.
2:
"And ye are puffed up, and have not rather mourned, that he that hath done this deed might be taken away from among you." The witnesses note the Corinthians' pride instead of mourning. Gill notes they were puffed up — with the gifts and eloquence of their teachers, or one party against another, or even in their outward flourishing condition — when they ought to have been humbled. The witnesses note Paul rebuking the Corinthians for being puffed up (proud, self-confident) instead of mourning over the scandal, and for not having put away the offender; the misplaced pride that gloried while the church was defiled, the failure to grieve over the sin, and the neglect of the discipline that should have removed the wicked man; the pride that should have been grief. The witnesses note ye are puffed up. The witnesses note have not rather mourned.

The witnesses note the Corinthians puffed up instead of mourning — the misplaced pride that gloried while the church was defiled, neglecting discipline.

So the witnesses make the verse the Corinthians' pride instead of mourning. The lesson the expositors draw is the misplaced pride that gloried while the church was defiled, and the failure to grieve and to discipline. The witnesses note the pride: "ye are puffed up" — Gill notes they were puffed up — with their teachers' gifts, their parties, or their flourishing condition; the puffed up; the pride; the self-confidence; the glorying; the inflated state. The expositors observe the failure to mourn: "and have not rather mourned" — they had not mourned over the scandal — the sin in their midst; the failure to mourn; the no grief; the absence of sorrow; the not-mourning; the unmoved church. The witnesses press the misplaced pride: the Corinthians gloried (in their gifts, teachers, condition) while the church was defiled by the scandal — the misplaced pride; the glorying amid defilement; the pride while the sin festered; the wrong response; the pride instead of grief. The expositors draw the lesson on the duty to mourn over sin: the church should have mourned over the sin — grieving over the dishonour to Christ, the defilement of the body, the danger to the offender; the duty to mourn; the grief over sin; the sorrow for the scandal; the mourning that becomes the holy; the godly grief. The witnesses observe the neglect of discipline: "that he that hath done this deed might be taken away from among you" — they had not put away the offender; the neglect; the discipline failed; the wicked man not removed; the purging neglected; the offender tolerated. The expositors note the connection of mourning and discipline: true mourning over the sin would have led to the discipline — the putting away of the offender; the connection; the grief issuing in action; the mourning and the purging; the sorrow that acts; the grief that disciplines. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye are puffed up, the have not rather mourned, and the pride that should have been grief. The verse thus records the Corinthians' pride instead of mourning — teaching the misplaced pride that gloried while the church was defiled, and the failure to grieve and to discipline.
3:
"For I verily, as absent in body, but present in spirit, have judged already, as though I were present, concerning him that hath thus done this deed," The witnesses note Paul's apostolic judgment of the case. Barnes notes Paul, whatever it might cost him and however they esteemed his interference, had adjudged this case to be so flagrant as to demand the exercise of discipline. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, though absent in body, he was present in spirit and had already judged the case as if present; the apostolic authority exercised at a distance — Paul, by the Spirit and his apostolic office, rendering judgment on the offender though far away, and calling the church to act on it; the judgment of the apostle, present in spirit though absent in body. The witnesses note absent in body, present in spirit. The witnesses note judged already.

The witnesses note Paul's apostolic judgment — absent in body but present in spirit, he had already judged the flagrant case.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's apostolic judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostolic authority exercised at a distance, Paul rendering judgment on the offender though far away. The witnesses note the absence and presence: "I verily, as absent in body, but present in spirit" — Paul was absent in body (not at Corinth) but present in spirit; the absence; the presence in spirit; the apostle far yet near; the spiritual presence; the absent-present. The expositors observe the judgment: "have judged already, as though I were present" — Paul had already judged the case, as if present; Barnes notes he had adjudged the case so flagrant as to demand discipline; the judgment; the adjudging; the verdict; the case decided; the apostle's judgment. The witnesses press the apostolic authority: Paul exercised his apostolic authority at a distance — rendering judgment on the offender though far away, by the Spirit and his office; the authority; the apostolic office; the judgment at a distance; the power exercised; the apostle's warrant. The expositors draw the lesson on the present-in-spirit judgment: Paul, present in spirit, judged the case — his apostolic discernment and authority reaching across the distance; the present in spirit; the discernment; the judgment by the Spirit; the authority across the miles; the spiritual presence. The witnesses observe the flagrant case: the case was flagrant — so clear and gross that Paul could judge it even absent; the flagrant; the clear case; the gross sin; the evident wickedness; the case beyond doubt. The expositors note the calling of the church to act: Paul's judgment was to be acted on by the church — the apostle judging, the church executing; the calling to act; the church to carry out; the judgment to be applied; the discipline to follow; the church's part. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the absent in body present in spirit, the judged already, and the apostolic judgment. The verse thus records Paul's apostolic judgment — teaching the apostolic authority exercised at a distance, the offender judged though Paul was far away.
4:
"In the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, when ye are gathered together, and my spirit, with the power of our Lord Jesus Christ," The witnesses note the solemn manner of the discipline. Gill notes the act would be done "in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ" — by his command, power, and authority, and for his glory, in whose name all such actions were performed. The witnesses note Paul directing that the discipline be done in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ, with the church gathered together, his spirit present, and the power of the Lord Jesus; the solemn, authoritative manner of the church's act — done in Christ's name and authority, by the assembled congregation, with the apostle's concurring spirit and the power of Christ present; the discipline in Christ's name and power. The witnesses note in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ. The witnesses note gathered together with power.

The witnesses note the solemn manner of discipline — in the name of the Lord Jesus, the church gathered, with the power of Christ present.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn manner of the discipline. The lesson the expositors draw is the authoritative, solemn manner of the church's act — done in Christ's name and power, by the assembled congregation. The witnesses note the name of Christ: "in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ" — Gill notes the act would be done in Christ's name — by his command, power, and authority, for his glory; the name of Christ; the authority; the command; the warrant; the in Christ's name. The expositors observe the assembled church: "when ye are gathered together" — the discipline was to be done by the church gathered together — the assembled congregation; the gathering; the assembly; the congregation; the church together; the corporate act. The witnesses press the apostle's concurring spirit: "and my spirit" — Paul's spirit was present (concurring, joining in the act); the apostle's spirit; the concurrence; the joining; the present spirit; the apostle with them. The expositors draw the lesson on the power of Christ: "with the power of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the act was done with the power of the Lord Jesus — his authority and might present; the power; the authority of Christ; the might; the power present; the Lord's power. The witnesses observe the solemnity of the act: the discipline was solemn — done in Christ's name, by the assembled church, with the apostle's spirit and Christ's power; the solemnity; the gravity; the authoritative act; the solemn discipline; the weighty action. The expositors note the corporate and authoritative nature: the discipline was corporate (the church gathered) and authoritative (in Christ's name and power) — not a private or light matter; the corporate; the authoritative; the church's act; the weighty discipline; the solemn assembly. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, the gathered together with power, and the solemn manner of discipline. The verse thus records the solemn manner of the discipline — teaching the authoritative, solemn manner of the church's act, done in Christ's name and power by the assembled congregation.
5:
"To deliver such an one unto Satan for the destruction of the flesh, that the spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord Jesus." The witnesses note the delivering to Satan and its purpose. Gill distinguishes this from the church's act of excommunication — "delivering to Satan" being an apostolic act, with miraculous power, by which the offender was given over to Satan for bodily affliction (the destruction of the flesh); whereas the putting away (v.13) was the church's act. The witnesses note Paul directing that the offender be delivered to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, that the spirit might be saved in the day of the Lord; the severe but merciful discipline — the offender given over to Satan's buffeting (and, with the apostolic power, to bodily affliction) for the mortifying of the flesh, with the saving aim that his spirit be saved at last; the discipline aimed at salvation. The witnesses note deliver unto Satan. The witnesses note that the spirit may be saved.

The witnesses note the offender delivered to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, that the spirit may be saved — the severe but merciful, saving discipline.

So the witnesses make the verse the delivering to Satan and its saving purpose. The lesson the expositors draw is the severe but merciful discipline, the offender given over for the mortifying of the flesh with the saving aim. The witnesses note the delivering: "to deliver such an one unto Satan" — Gill distinguishes this from the church's excommunication — "delivering to Satan" an apostolic act, with miraculous power, giving the offender over to Satan; the delivering; the giving over to Satan; the apostolic act; the handing to Satan; the severe sentence. The expositors weigh the meaning: the witnesses note "deliver to Satan" embraces the apostolic, extraordinary act (giving over to Satan's power for bodily affliction, as Job, with the miraculous power of the apostle) and, with it, the church's putting out of the offender into the world (Satan's domain) outside the church; the delivering to Satan; the affliction; the exclusion; the extraordinary and the ordinary. The witnesses press the destruction of the flesh: "for the destruction of the flesh" — the witnesses weigh the sense — the bodily affliction (the body chastened, perhaps to physical suffering or death), or the mortifying of the sinful flesh/nature (the corruption subdued); the destruction of the flesh; the bodily chastening or the mortifying of sin; the affliction; the subduing of the flesh. The expositors draw the lesson on the saving aim: "that the spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord Jesus" — the aim of the severe discipline was the offender's salvation — that his spirit be saved at the last day; the saving aim; the discipline for salvation; the severity for mercy; the spirit saved; the merciful end. The witnesses observe the mercy in the severity: the discipline, though severe, was merciful — aimed not at destruction but at the offender's repentance and final salvation; the mercy; the severity for good; the discipline that saves; the chastening for restoration; the love in the rod. The expositors note the distinction of the apostolic and church acts: Gill distinguishes the apostolic delivering to Satan (extraordinary, with bodily affliction) from the church's excommunication (the putting away, v.13); the distinction; the apostolic and the ordinary; the two acts; the extraordinary power; the church's duty. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the deliver unto Satan, the spirit saved, and the saving aim of discipline. The verse thus records the offender delivered to Satan for the destruction of the flesh — teaching the severe but merciful discipline aimed at the offender's salvation.
6:
"Your glorying is not good. Know ye not that a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump?" The witnesses note the warning against the spreading defilement of sin. Gill notes their glorying — in their condition, their riches, their ministers, or even in conniving at the sin — was not good, but criminal and dangerous. Barnes notes their boasting, their confidence as if they were eminent in purity. The witnesses note Paul reproving their glorying and warning that a little leaven leavens the whole lump; the danger of tolerated sin — the misplaced pride rebuked, and the principle that one unjudged sin (like leaven) spreads its corrupting influence through the whole church if not purged; the contagion of sin. The witnesses note your glorying is not good. The witnesses note a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump.

The witnesses note the warning that a little leaven leavens the whole lump — the contagion of tolerated sin spreading through the church.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning against the contagion of sin. The lesson the expositors draw is that one unjudged sin, like leaven, spreads its corrupting influence through the whole church if not purged. The witnesses note the rebuke: "your glorying is not good" — Gill notes their glorying (in their condition, ministers, or conniving at the sin) was not good but criminal; Barnes notes their boasting, their confidence as if pure; the rebuke; the glorying reproved; the pride condemned; the boasting wrong; the not good. The expositors observe the proverb: "a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump" — a little leaven (yeast) leavens (spreads through, sours) the whole lump of dough; the proverb; the leaven; the spreading; the contagion; the little affecting the whole. The witnesses press the contagion of sin: as a little leaven spreads through the whole lump, so one tolerated sin spreads its corrupting influence through the whole church; the contagion; the spreading sin; the corruption of the whole; the leaven of sin; the influence of the unjudged. The expositors draw the lesson on the danger of tolerated sin: tolerated sin is dangerous — it spreads, corrupts, defiles the whole; the danger; the toleration perilous; the sin that spreads; the unjudged corruption; the leaven unpurged. The witnesses observe the necessity of purging: since a little leaven leavens the whole, the sin must be purged — the offender put away (v.7); the necessity; the purging; the removal; the discipline required; the leaven cast out. The expositors note the principle for the church: the church must judge and purge sin — lest, like leaven, it corrupt the whole; the principle; the church's duty; the purging of sin; the discipline; the holiness of the body. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the your glorying is not good, the little leaven, and the contagion of tolerated sin. The verse thus records the warning against the contagion of sin — teaching that one unjudged sin, like leaven, spreads through the whole church if not purged.
7:
"Purge out therefore the old leaven, that ye may be a new lump, as ye are unleavened. For even Christ our passover is sacrificed for us:" The witnesses note the call to purge the leaven and the typology of Christ our passover. Gill notes "purge out the old leaven" means removing the incestuous person (the act of excommunication, the church's duty), the allusion being to the Jews' strict search for leaven before the passover. The witnesses note Paul calling the church to purge out the old leaven that they may be a new lump, as they are unleavened, for Christ our passover is sacrificed for us; the duty to remove the corrupting sin (and the offender), grounded in the typology — Christ the true Passover lamb sacrificed for his people, who, like the unleavened bread of the feast, are to be pure; the purging and the Passover. The witnesses note purge out the old leaven. The witnesses note Christ our passover.

The witnesses note the call to purge the old leaven, grounded in Christ our Passover sacrificed for us — the purity that befits the redeemed.

So the witnesses make the verse the purging of the leaven and the Passover typology. The lesson the expositors draw is the duty to purge the corrupting sin, grounded in the typology of Christ the true Passover. The witnesses note the call: "purge out therefore the old leaven" — Gill notes this means removing the incestuous person (the church's act of excommunication), the allusion to the Jews' search for leaven before the passover; the purging; the removal; the old leaven cast out; the offender removed; the cleansing. The expositors observe the new lump: "that ye may be a new lump, as ye are unleavened" — purging the leaven, they would be a new lump, as they are (in principle, by Christ) unleavened; the new lump; the purity; the unleavened state; the cleansed church; the new dough. The witnesses press the Passover typology: "for even Christ our passover is sacrificed for us" — Christ is our Passover, sacrificed for us — the true Passover Lamb, whose blood redeems and shelters his people; the typology; Christ the Passover; the Lamb sacrificed; the true passover; the redemption. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ the true Passover: as the passover lamb was slain and its blood sheltered Israel, so Christ, our Passover, was sacrificed — his blood redeeming and sheltering his people; the true Passover; the Lamb of God (John 1:29); the blood that shelters; the redemption by Christ; the antitype. The witnesses observe the purity that befits the redeemed: as the passover was kept with unleavened bread (all leaven purged), so the redeemed, sheltered by Christ's blood, are to be pure — the leaven of sin purged; the purity; the unleavened life; the holiness that befits the redeemed; the purging grounded in redemption; the pure feast. The expositors note the ground of the purging in the sacrifice: the purging is grounded in the sacrifice — since Christ our Passover is slain, his people are to be pure, purging the leaven; the ground; the sacrifice the motive; the redemption requiring purity; the cross and the cleansing; the holiness from the sacrifice. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the purge out the old leaven, the Christ our passover, and the purity that befits the redeemed. The verse thus records the call to purge the leaven, grounded in Christ our Passover — teaching the duty to remove the corrupting sin, grounded in the typology of the true Passover sacrificed for us.
8:
"Therefore let us keep the feast, not with old leaven, neither with the leaven of malice and wickedness; but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth." The witnesses note the call to keep the feast with sincerity and truth. Barnes notes the language is drawn from the paschal feast; it does not mean literally the passover (ceased) nor particularly the Lord's Supper, but the whole Christian life — the keeping of a perpetual feast. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers to keep the feast — not with the old leaven, nor the leaven of malice and wickedness, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth; the whole Christian life as a perpetual Passover feast, kept by purging the leaven of sin (malice and wickedness) and living in sincerity and truth; the feast of the holy life. The witnesses note keep the feast. The witnesses note sincerity and truth.

The witnesses note the call to keep the feast with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth — the whole Christian life a perpetual Passover.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to keep the feast with sincerity and truth. The lesson the expositors draw is the whole Christian life as a perpetual Passover feast, kept by purging the leaven of sin and living in sincerity. The witnesses note the feast: "let us keep the feast" — Barnes notes the language is from the paschal feast; not literally the passover nor particularly the Lord's Supper, but the whole Christian life; the feast; the perpetual feast; the Christian life; the keeping of the feast; the holy living. The expositors observe the leaven to be excluded: "not with old leaven, neither with the leaven of malice and wickedness" — the feast is not to be kept with the old leaven (the former corruption), nor the leaven of malice and wickedness (sin, ill-will, vice); the leaven excluded; the old corruption; the malice and wickedness; the sin purged; the leaven cast out. The witnesses press the unleavened bread: "but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth" — the feast is kept with the unleavened bread of sincerity (purity of motive, single-heartedness) and truth (integrity, honesty); the unleavened bread; the sincerity; the truth; the purity; the integrity. The expositors draw the lesson on the perpetual feast: as Israel kept the seven-day feast of unleavened bread after the passover, so the redeemed keep a perpetual feast — the whole Christian life lived in purity, all leaven of sin purged; the perpetual feast; the unleavened life; the continual holiness; the feast of the redeemed; the holy living. The witnesses observe sincerity and truth: the feast is kept with sincerity (purity, single-heartedness) and truth (integrity) — the marks of the holy life; the sincerity; the truth; the purity of heart; the integrity; the holy marks. The expositors note the contrast of leaven and unleavened: the contrast — the leaven of malice and wickedness against the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth; the sin against the holiness; the corruption against the purity; the leaven and the unleavened; the two ways. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the keep the feast, the sincerity and truth, and the whole life a perpetual Passover. The verse thus records the call to keep the feast with sincerity and truth — teaching the whole Christian life as a perpetual Passover, kept by purging the leaven of sin and living in sincerity.
9:
"I wrote unto you in an epistle not to company with fornicators:" The witnesses note Paul's reference to a former instruction. Chrysostom notes that, since Paul had said "purge out the old leaven," they might surmise they should avoid all fornicators (even those outside) — for if a sinner imparts mischief to those who have not sinned, much more should one keep away from those without; under this impression Paul clarifies. The witnesses note Paul referring to a former letter in which he had instructed them not to company with fornicators; the previous instruction (in a now-lost earlier letter) against keeping company with the immoral — which the Corinthians had misunderstood, prompting Paul's clarification (v.10-11); the former instruction clarified. The witnesses note I wrote unto you. The witnesses note not to company with fornicators.

The witnesses note Paul's reference to a former letter against companying with fornicators — the instruction the Corinthians had misunderstood.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's reference to a former instruction. The lesson the expositors draw is the previous instruction against keeping company with the immoral, which the Corinthians had misunderstood. The witnesses note the former letter: "I wrote unto you in an epistle" — Paul refers to a former letter (a now-lost earlier epistle) he had written; the former letter; the previous epistle; the earlier writing; the prior instruction; the lost letter. The expositors observe the instruction: "not to company with fornicators" — in that letter Paul had instructed them not to company (keep company, associate) with fornicators; the instruction; the not companying; the separation; the avoiding; the former command. The witnesses press the misunderstanding: Chrysostom notes the Corinthians might have surmised they should avoid all fornicators (even those outside the church) — for if a sinner imparts mischief, much more should one keep from those without; the misunderstanding; the wrong inference; the over-application; the confusion; the misread instruction. The expositors draw the lesson on the clarification to follow: Paul refers to the former instruction to clarify it (v.10-11) — he did not mean the fornicators of the world (else they would have to leave the world), but those within the church; the clarification; the correction; the right sense; the instruction explained; the meaning clarified. The witnesses observe the existence of a former letter: the verse shows Paul had written a former letter to the Corinthians (now lost) — not all the apostle's writings were preserved in Scripture; the former letter; the lost epistle; the prior writing; the unpreserved letter; the historical note. The expositors note the care to be rightly understood: Paul takes care to be rightly understood — clarifying the former instruction lest it be misapplied; the care; the clarification; the right understanding; the avoiding of misapplication; the apostle's precision. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I wrote unto you, the not to company with fornicators, and the instruction to be clarified. The verse thus records Paul's reference to a former instruction — teaching the previous command against companying with the immoral, which the Corinthians had misunderstood and which Paul now clarifies.
10:
"Yet not altogether with the fornicators of this world, or with the covetous, or extortioners, or with idolaters; for then must ye needs go out of the world." The witnesses note the clarification — not the world's fornicators. Gill notes "the fornicators of this world" are worldly, carnal men, never called out of it; Paul's prohibition was not to be understood of such, for then one must leave the world altogether. The witnesses note Paul clarifying that he did not mean the fornicators, covetous, extortioners, and idolaters of the world — for to avoid all such, one would have to leave the world entirely; the necessary commerce with sinners in the world — the believer must live among the unconverted, and cannot wholly avoid them, else he must depart from the world; the limit of separation. The witnesses note the fornicators of this world. The witnesses note go out of the world.

The witnesses note the clarification — not the world's sinners, for then one must leave the world; the necessary commerce with the unconverted.

So the witnesses make the verse the clarification about the world's sinners. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer must live among the unconverted and cannot wholly avoid them, else he must depart from the world. The witnesses note the clarification: "yet not altogether with the fornicators of this world" — Gill notes "the fornicators of this world" are worldly, carnal men never called out of it; Paul did not mean such; the clarification; the not the world's sinners; the correction; the right sense; the world's fornicators. The expositors observe the catalogue: "or with the covetous, or extortioners, or with idolaters" — the catalogue of the world's sinners — fornicators, covetous, extortioners, idolaters; the catalogue; the world's vices; the sinners of the world; the various wicked; the unconverted. The witnesses press the impossibility of total avoidance: "for then must ye needs go out of the world" — to avoid all such, one would have to leave the world entirely; the impossibility; the necessary commerce; the cannot wholly avoid; the must leave the world; the unavoidable contact. The expositors draw the lesson on the believer in the world: the believer must live among the unconverted — in business, society, daily life — and cannot wholly separate from them; the believer in the world; the necessary commerce; the living among sinners; the not of the world but in it (John 17:15); the unavoidable contact. The witnesses observe the limit of separation: the separation Paul commanded was not from the world's sinners (impossible) but from the immoral within the church (v.11); the limit; the separation bounded; the not from the world; the within the church; the right separation. The expositors note the balance: the believer is to be separate (holy) yet in the world (for witness and necessary commerce) — not withdrawing into a monastery, but living holy among sinners; the balance; the in but not of; the holy yet present; the witness in the world; the separation and the commerce. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the fornicators of this world, the go out of the world, and the necessary commerce with the unconverted. The verse thus records the clarification about the world's sinners — teaching that the believer must live among the unconverted and cannot wholly avoid them.
11:
"But now I have written unto you not to keep company, if any man that is called a brother be a fornicator, or covetous, or an idolater, or a railer, or a drunkard, or an extortioner; with such an one no not to eat." The witnesses note the discipline of the immoral within the church. Gill notes this explains the former instruction — the separation is from one "called a brother" (a church member) who lives in such sin. The witnesses note Paul instructing that they not keep company with any man called a brother who is a fornicator, covetous, idolater, railer, drunkard, or extortioner — with such a one not even to eat; the discipline of the immoral church member — the believer who lives in open, unrepented sin to be shunned (refused even table fellowship), that he be ashamed and the church kept pure; the separation from the sinning brother. The witnesses note any man called a brother. The witnesses note no not to eat.

The witnesses note the discipline of the immoral church member — the brother who lives in open sin to be shunned, even refused table fellowship.

So the witnesses make the verse the discipline of the immoral within the church. The lesson the expositors draw is the shunning of the believer who lives in open, unrepented sin, that he be ashamed and the church kept pure. The witnesses note the object: "if any man that is called a brother be a fornicator" — Gill notes the separation is from one "called a brother" (a church member) who lives in such sin; the brother; the church member; the professing believer; the called brother; the one within. The expositors observe the catalogue of sins: "or covetous, or an idolater, or a railer, or a drunkard, or an extortioner" — the catalogue — fornication, covetousness, idolatry, railing, drunkenness, extortion; the catalogue; the sins; the open vices; the disqualifying sins; the wickedness. The witnesses press the shunning: "with such an one no not to eat" — with such a one the believers are not to keep company, not even to eat; the shunning; the no company; the refused fellowship; the not eating; the separation. The expositors draw the lesson on the discipline of the brother in sin: the church member who lives in open, unrepented sin is to be shunned — refused fellowship, even table fellowship; the discipline; the shunning; the brother in sin; the separation; the withdrawn fellowship. The witnesses observe the purpose — that he be ashamed: the shunning aims at the offender's shame and repentance — that, ashamed, he may turn (2 Thessalonians 3:14); the purpose; the shame; the repentance; the restoration aimed; the discipline for good. The expositors note the keeping of the church pure: the discipline also keeps the church pure — the leaven purged, the holiness maintained; the purity; the church kept clean; the leaven removed; the holiness; the body guarded. The witnesses observe the open and unrepented sin: the discipline is for open, persistent, unrepented sin — not the occasional fault, but the settled, scandalous course; the open sin; the persistent; the unrepented; the scandalous course; the settled wickedness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the any man called a brother, the no not to eat, and the discipline of the sinning brother. The verse thus records the discipline of the immoral within the church — teaching the shunning of the believer who lives in open sin, that he be ashamed and the church kept pure.
12:
"For what have I to do to judge them also that are without? do not ye judge them that are within?" The witnesses note the church's jurisdiction over its own. Gill notes "to judge" here is to admonish, reprove, censure, and condemn; Lightfoot discusses the phrase, the sense being that the church's judgment extends to its own members, not to outsiders. The witnesses note Paul asking what he (or the church) has to do to judge those without (outside the church), since their concern is to judge those within; the limit of the church's disciplinary jurisdiction — the church judges (disciplines) its own members, not the men of the world, who are outside its bounds; the jurisdiction over the church's own. The witnesses note judge them that are without. The witnesses note judge them that are within.

The witnesses note the church's jurisdiction over its own — judging those within, not those without; the limit of disciplinary authority.

So the witnesses make the verse the church's jurisdiction over its own. The lesson the expositors draw is that the church's discipline extends to its own members, not to outsiders. The witnesses note the question: "what have I to do to judge them also that are without?" — Gill notes "to judge" is to admonish, reprove, censure; Paul asks what he (or the church) has to do to judge those without (outside the church); the question; the judging of outsiders; the jurisdiction; the limit; the those without. The expositors observe the church's concern: "do not ye judge them that are within?" — the church's concern is to judge those within (its own members); the within; the church's own; the members; the jurisdiction over its own; the internal discipline. The witnesses press the limit of jurisdiction: the church's disciplinary jurisdiction extends to its own members (those within), not to the men of the world (those without); the limit; the jurisdiction bounded; the own members; the not outsiders; the church's bounds. The expositors draw the lesson on the church judging its own: the church is to judge (discipline) its own members — admonishing, censuring, excluding the unrepentant; the church's duty; the discipline of members; the judging of the own; the internal authority; the church's responsibility. The witnesses observe the not judging outsiders: the church does not judge (discipline) the men of the world — they are outside its bounds, under God's judgment (v.13); the not outsiders; the world not judged by the church; the outsiders left to God; the no jurisdiction over the world; the limit. The expositors note the proper sphere of discipline: the proper sphere of church discipline is the church itself — its own members; the proper sphere; the church's own; the jurisdiction; the bounds of discipline; the internal sphere. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the judge them that are without, the judge them that are within, and the church's jurisdiction over its own. The verse thus records the church's jurisdiction over its own — teaching that the church's discipline extends to its own members, not to outsiders.
13:
"But them that are without God judgeth. Therefore put away from among yourselves that wicked person." The witnesses note that God judges outsiders, and the command to put away the wicked. Gill notes God will judge those without in the great day; though they fall not under the church's censure, they shall not go unpunished, but be called to account. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God judges those without, and commanding the church to put away from among themselves the wicked person; the assurance that outsiders are not unjudged (God will judge them at the last) and the plain command for the church to excommunicate the unrepentant offender — the putting away the church's own act, the discipline that purges the wicked from the body; the command to excommunicate. The witnesses note God judgeth them without. The witnesses note put away the wicked person.

The witnesses note God judging outsiders and the command to put away the wicked person — the church's act of excommunication.

So the witnesses make the verse God's judgment of outsiders and the command to excommunicate. The lesson the expositors draw is that outsiders are judged by God, and the church is to put away the unrepentant offender. The witnesses note the judgment of outsiders: "them that are without God judgeth" — Gill notes God will judge those without in the great day; though they fall not under the church's censure, they shall not go unpunished; the judgment; God judges; the outsiders judged; the great day; the divine reckoning. The expositors observe the assurance: the outsiders are not unjudged — God will judge them at the last; the assurance; the not unjudged; the divine judgment; the reckoning to come; the sinners not escaping. The witnesses press the command: "put away from among yourselves that wicked person" — the church is commanded to put away (excommunicate, remove) the wicked person from among them; the command; the putting away; the excommunication; the removal; the wicked person cast out. The expositors draw the lesson on the church's act of excommunication: the putting away is the church's own act — the excommunication of the unrepentant offender; the church's act; the excommunication; the removal; the discipline; the casting out. The witnesses observe the purging of the body: the command purges the wicked from the body — the leaven removed, the church kept pure; the purging; the body cleansed; the leaven cast out; the holiness; the church purified. The expositors note the source — Deuteronomy: the command "put away... that wicked person" echoes the repeated command of Deuteronomy ("put away the evil from among you," Deuteronomy 17:7); the source; the Deuteronomy echo; the scriptural command; the old covenant pattern; the purging of evil. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God judgeth them without, the put away the wicked person, and the command to excommunicate. The verse thus records God's judgment of outsiders and the command to put away the wicked — teaching that outsiders are judged by God, and the church is to excommunicate the unrepentant offender.
